label: False Imprisonment
scenario_patterns: A retail store security guard detains a shopper in a back room for hours based on a mistaken suspicion of theft
An employer locks an employee in a meeting room and refuses to let them leave until they sign a resignation letter
A private security firm restrains a person at a condominium guardhouse without lawful authority
A creditor physically blocks a debtor from leaving a room until the debtor agrees to pay
legal_tests: Total restraint of the plaintiff's liberty — the plaintiff must be confined with no reasonable means of escape
The restraint must be without lawful justification
The defendant must have intended the act of confinement (intent to harm is not required)
The plaintiff need not be aware of the confinement at the time for the tort to be established
Any residual exit route must be reasonable — the plaintiff is not required to risk injury or indignity to escape
common_fact_patterns: Shoplifting detention by retail security that exceeds reasonable scope or duration
Wrongful detention by private security at condominiums or commercial premises
Employer locking employees in rooms during disputes or termination processes
Unlawful arrest or detention by private persons exceeding citizen's arrest powers
Immigration-related wrongful detention of foreign workers by employers
sg_case_references: Seah Kok Meng v Public Prosecutor [2001] SGHC 306 — elements of false imprisonment discussed
Lee Tat Development Pte Ltd v Management Corporation Strata Title Plan No 301 [2009] SGCA 16 — trespass to person including false imprisonment
Lim Thian Lai v Public Prosecutor [2006] 2 SLR 155 — wrongful confinement and restraint of liberty
James Raj s/o Arokiasamy v Public Prosecutor [2014] SGHC 10 — unlawful restraint principles applicable in civil context
party_roles: employer vicariously liable for employee's wrongful detention
defences: Lawful authority — detention was pursuant to a lawful arrest under the Criminal Procedure Code
Reasonable condition — the plaintiff was free to leave upon satisfying a reasonable condition
Citizen's arrest under s 66 CPC — reasonable suspicion of a seizable offence and the person was promptly handed to police
Statutory authority — detention authorised by specific legislation (e.g., Mental Health Act)
